text: A seton is visible at the start of the procedure. The surgeon places a Lone Star retractor to expose the surgical field and infiltrates the vaginal wall with an adrenaline solution.

The surgeon removes the ceton and inserts a fistula probe into the fistula track. Methylene blue is injected into the track, and stay sutures are placed to expose the fistula track for further dissection.

Three stay sutures are placed above the fistula track, with the suture material hooked around the lone star retractor to enhance exposure. The third suture is carefully positioned adjacent to the fistula probe and similarly hooked around the retractor.
Three stay sutures are placed inferior to the vaginal opening of the fistula tract, enhancing exposure of the tract for subsequent dissection and removal.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection around the vaginal opening of the fistula, with the fistula probe remaining in position to facilitate movement and exposure of the fistula tract for further dissection.

The surgeon uses a clip on the fistula track to facilitate dissection, pulling on the track to sharply dissect down to the pouch opening of the fistula. The fistula track is visible, showing its short length.
The surgeon uses a suction device to define the edges of the fistula openings. The internal opening on the pouch side is then closed using dissolvable monofilament suture material.

A finger is placed in the rectum to expose and define the internal opening on the pouch side of the fistula. The fistula is located at the site of a previous staple anastomosis, with visible erosion of staples through adjacent tissues contributing to the formation of vaginal fistulas.
The surgeon defines the tissues on the vaginal side of the fistula, encountering difficulty in dissection on the left side due to the presence of a previous Martius flap.

The surgeon observes the remaining tissues of the Martius flap, which are freed to cover the internal opening of the fistula. On the right side of the vagina, the tissues appear more pliable due to the absence of a Martius flap.

The surgeon dissects to free up the vaginal musculature and attached levators, providing muscle tissue to close the internal opening of the fistula tract. The vaginal mucosa is elevated with the insertion of local anesthetic.

The surgical field is relatively bloody, requiring the use of suction for clear visualization. The surgeon ensures good exposure and lighting to aid in the dissection of the vaginal musculature, aiming to minimize tension through full mobilization.
The surgeon continues dissection in a cephalic direction, freeing up tissue to ensure the interlobe of the fistula is fully closed by the vaginal musculature.

The surgeon places a suture at the top end of the dissection to bring muscle across and close the upper aspect of the fistula. Further dissection of the left Martius flap is visible, appearing yellow in the center of the image, as it is used to suture over the internal defect and further close the internal opening of the fistula.

The surgeon examines the vaginal musculature, preparing to insert sutures to approximate the two sides, ensuring the muscle is well-nourished and vascularized to effectively close over the previous site of the fistula tract.

The surgeon uses a pyretractor to expose the vault of the vagina, ensuring the vaginal mucosa is closed along its length with monofilament sutures to minimize infection risk and bury the muscular repair.
The surgeon completes the closure of the vaginal mucosa using monofilament sutures. The operation concludes with the insertion of a urinary catheter and a vaginal pack soaked in estrogen cream.
